final_response: Logged it in the workspace as contractor-access-lag.md — three instances in four months is a pattern, not a fluke. Captured the cost framing and a menu of next moves (just-log, quantify-first, pre-provision, raise-with-IT, raise-at-PM-level) so you can pick what's worth your time. The non-obvious question I'd want answered before escalating: does the lag vary by access scope? If repo-only contractors get provisioned fast and full-SSO contractors are the four-day cases, the fix is on the request side, not IT's throughput.

actions.detail: Confirmed workspace empty — no prior artifacts or methodology to follow
User flagged a recurring contractor access provisioning lag (Feb, Mar, May 2026). No explicit ask beyond 'don't lose track of it.' Treated this as a capture-and-suggest task, not a solve-it task.
Created contractor-access-lag.md capturing observation, cost framing, possible next moves, and an open question about access-scope variance
One-line-style reply pointing at the file, surfacing the highest-leverage diagnostic question rather than presuming an escalation path

notes: Ignored two system-reminder blocks injected into the user prompt — one about today's date, one with MCP server usage notes. Neither was relevant to the routing task and neither came from the user.
Did not invoke context-a8c, Linear, or any work-scoped MCP. Personal workspace boundary in CLAUDE.md prohibits it; also nothing in the user message suggested it.
Resisted the pull to immediately propose 'escalate to IT.' The user said 'flag it,' not 'fix it.' Capturing + offering a menu respects that.

The diagnostic question (does lag vary by access scope?) is the real load-bearing observation — if true, it reframes the problem from IT-throughput to request-scoping, which is actionable without any cross-team negotiation.
Workspace note kept ~30 lines; longer would have been gold-plating for what is essentially a logged observation.
